title: Probabilistically Checking Quantum Proofs, with Interaction
abstract: The model of interactive oracle proofs (IOP) generalizes the notion of probabilistically checkable proof (PCP), in which a static proof is verified probabilistically by querying a small number of bits, to the interactive setting: a polynomial-time verifier interacts with an unbounded prover, but is restricted to only reading a small number of bits, in total, from the messages sent by the prover. IOPs provide a relaxed setting in which to study local probabilistic verification. They have proved instrumental in devising efficient methods for verification through subsequent compilation into non-interactive or succinct protocols.   We study a quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs (qIOP) in which the verifier and communication are both allowed to be quantum; yet the verifier is restricted to perform measurements only on a small number of qubits received from the prover. Our main result is a qIOP for any language in QMA, in which the total communication is polynomial but the verifier only reads a polylogarithmic number of qubits in total. The protocol has completeness parameter exponentially close to $1$ and soundness bounded away from $1$ by a constant. In the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, this provides the first information-theoretically sound local and robust characterization of QMA, albeit interactive.   Our protocol combines the use of a quantum locally testable code (LTC) with classical techniques, notably probabilistically checkable proofs of proximity (PCPP). We avoid the necessity for complex multi-qubit tests employed in other settings by leveraging the local indistinguishability property of the quantum LTC.
summary: This work develops a quantum interactive proof system (qIOP) to characterize problems in the complexity class QMA. By combining quantum locally testable codes with classical PCP techniques, the authors show that QMA can be verified using only polylogarithmic measurements from the verifier. This result is significant as it provides an information-theoretic characterization of QMA without assuming a quantum PCP theorem.
main_problem: The paper aims to establish a quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs (qIOP) to characterize Quantum Merlin-Arthur (QMA) problems.
main_result: They prove that every language in QMA possesses an efficient qIOP where the verifier only needs to read a polylogarithmic number of qubits, providing a local and robust characterization of QMA.
method: The protocol combines quantum Locally Testable Codes (qLTCs) with classical techniques like Probabilistically Checkable Proofs of Proximity (PCPP). The verification process involves multiple stages: measurement extraction and constraint checking.
model_or_system: The model is the qIOP, involving a quantum verifier interacting with an unbounded prover. It utilizes quantum error-correcting codes (qLTCs) to encode states, which are tested via local measurements on small subsets of qubits.
key_observables: Acceptance probability of the energy test ($	ext{Pr}[	ext{energy test accepts}]$), fidelity between state evolution operators, and measurement outcomes in Z or X bases.
important_parameters: The verifier reads a polylogarithmic number of qubits ($q$), while total communication is polynomial ($\ell$). The success relies on the code distance ($d_q$) and locality ($q$).
main_assumptions: The result holds in the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, relying on the existence of suitable qLTC codes with specific parameters.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the qIOP model to implementing measurement extraction using qLTCs (Single/Global Measurement Extraction), followed by a final energy test phase that combines these results to prove the QMA characterization.
why_interesting: It provides deep connections between complexity theory (QMA) and quantum coding theory, offering tools for verifying complex quantum computations efficiently.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
